result:
I keep thinking about the moment between sleeping and waking, when the mind is liquid and the borders of the self have thinned until only a membrane of thought separates “I” from everything else. In that membrane my grandmother lives again, shelling peas on the porch of a house that was demolished years before I was born. She hums a tune I do not recognize, yet it smells of rain-soaked earth and old floorboards, and the shell-shocked cat who patrolled our apartment hallway when I was ten comes to curl against her ankles, purring like a bomb that forgot its war. Each expelled breath is a door, and I walk through.

The table of the world keeps getting bigger, even though the chairs around it disappear. I inherited areak from my father— that sudden, unstoppable spasm of laughter when confronted with futility— and I find it at 3 a.m. while reading instructions for how to grow bok choy in outer space. Thirty-two steps begin with “retrieve your nutrient aerogel,” end with “gently rotate counter-clockwise to simulate seasonal tilts.” The manual warns that overzealous spinning can cause spontaneous flowering, which sounds like the politest scandal possible. Somewhere, a cosmonaut romance novel writes itself.

Words, too, are seeds. At six I ate a dictionary, not metaphorically; the librarian caught me tearing pages of “L,” lapping “lumber, liminal, lozenge” like communion. She said books are sacred; I listened, but already I was digesting the sacred, converting lignin and ink into Henning-shaped muscle and mood. For years afterward I expected the encyclopedia’s continents to grow inside my ribs; instead I got a limp, an unexplained fondness for cover songs in minor keys, and the recurring dream of a train station whose every sign says “DEPART” in every language except the one I can read when awake.

They told us technology would sever us from mystery. They forgot that mystery regenerates like a hydra, with more heads each time. Try watching a live satellite feed of an empty cafeteria at noon above the Arctic Circle: chairs folded onto oval tables like the wings of grounded birds, light pouring diagonally through skylights onto linoleum that remembers every spilt bowl of soup since Sputnik. Nothing moves except the minute hand dreaming itself forward, and yet the scene feels inhabited, almost inhaling. You realize suspense does not require a protagonist.

At fourteen I stood on a dock in the Florida heat while an older boy explained the constancy of the speed of light with a bag of microwave popcorn. Each kernel, he said, is a future you steadily outrunning. The burning smell meant we were accelerating. I loved him the way one loves aquarium glass—close enough to touch, forever wet between. The instant I admitted this to myself, thunder cracked, and pellets of rain sent tourists skidding under tin roofs. Later that year he mailed me a postcard stamped NO FORWARDING ADDRESS: “All the kernels popped. Some nights at sea I hear them. Don’t wait up.” I never learned whether he meant the popcorn or the future.

Memory is not a library card; it’s origami made of smoke. Fold a sheet once and you never recover the original dimensions. Fold twice and your mother’s refugee story turns into a paper swan you hang from the rear-view mirror of the car you now own but which looks suspiciously like the one she fled in except the bullet holes have been fluffed into upholstery and the scent of gasoline replaced by lemon air freshener. Navigate by dead reckoning: night after night, inching west until you either reach the ocean or forget to.

Sometimes a single belt buckle survives— dull brass etched with a stag— and that buckle becomes the archive, the oldest undebatable fact. It once held someone upright; now it holds an entire century upright. What is history if not the spring mechanism that keeps our pants from pooling around our ankles while we stand in line for bread? You can study wars, treaties, the price of wheat, but the buckle keeps smirking on museum velvet, reminding you the past was inhabited, day to day, by bodies that took hunger personally.

Every city sells souvenirs of itself to itself. The Berlin bear terrifies me, not for politics but because it waves with both paws, swearing simultaneous allegiance and surrender. Tokyo’s Skytree keychain glows a sinister pink when moved, a lighthouse for lost robots. In Lagos Market the air smells of bruised mango and diesel; an auntie lifts my wrist, not to read my fortune but to check the time zone I’m broadcasting from grief. Souvenirs are promises that if you open them far enough from home they will re-constitute wherever you stand: a stories without visas. I have a drawer of them. On quiet nights they gossip in currencies I can almost count.

I think often of the word “whelm.” Not overwhelm, not underwhelm— just whelm, to be covered, not drowned, by water. The tide that reaches exactly to your chin before receding. Relationships like that: neither ordeal nor trifling. After my grandfather died, my grandmother began answering every question with “About half past.” What time is dinner? About half past. The news sounds dire. About half past. Eventually she inverted the joke: full sentences delivered only at 30-minute marks. The rest of the hour, silence. We learned to time our grief within those windows. Perhaps all rituals are just ways to keep the self half-whelmed, head above, heart below.

Modernity insists clarity is currency, but I am broke with blur. Zoom screens pixelate mid-sentence, and I can almost walk through the gaps— sideways into 1895 where a girl in Kraków sneezes and thereby invents an allergy to futures she will never taste. Love, too, pixelates; one day the face I adore dissolves into colored squares, and yet the voice continues describing garden plans as if borders did not exist. Pixelated voice and voiceless pixels share the same orbit, a binary planet system kept warm by mutual misreading. The screen freezes on the word “possibly,” and for seven real-time minutes everyone in the meeting remains suspended in that possible, scared to breathe and bump it into the impossible.

Once I heard a street preacher in Chicago proclaim, “The body is a key that unlocks nothing.” He was eating a peach while he said it, juice running down a wrist tattooed with barcodes. Somewhere between sentences he offered me a slice; that slice still exits, traversing my small intestine like a slow-motion protest march. I have never decided whether the fruit or the sentence digests me more thoroughly. When I die, bury me with seeds so that whatever sprouts will inherit at least one barcode.

There is a library on the moon kept in vacuum-sealed titanium canisters. Inside are not books but odors: petrichor, baking bread, the plastic of a brand-new doll, the inside of a subway car during first snowfall. They call it the Impossible Archive. Astronauts, after months breathing antiseptic air, are taken to the Chapel of Smells for moral resupply. They queue, record synesthetic memories triggered— chalk outlines of childhood kitchens, a lover’s shampoo flaring into the constellation Orion. Psychologists say the ritual cuts homesickness by forty percent, but the other sixty percent just crystallizes. They store those leftovers in orbit as radiant waste that sings to radios at two a.m., pull over lonely truckers mistaking it for golden oldies.

I want to live in an alphabet where “here” and “hear” swap letters and nobody minds. Where listening is the same as arriving, and leaving is spelled leaving. Every tongue already stutters toward that merger; accents are merely jurisdictions of longing. Sometimes when traffic lights click into their red lullaby, the sound word is the place itself: heart whispers and becomes a horizon.

The average cloud weighs about as much as a hundred elephants, the internet tells me at 4:02 a.m. between videos on how to fold fitted sheets and footage of beached whales being refloated. I scroll until the cloud slides into the whale and the sheet knots itself around both, forming a manta-ray made of linen and apology. The video loops again; the whale lifts off like a lazy parachute, taking the elephant-weight cloud with it. Offline, my neighbor sneezes exactly once, the sound ricocheting against refrigerators until it returns missing its initial “h.” Now the sneeze is “atcshoo,” a cousin from another dialect. We are all cousins in the dark.

My mother texts: “Do you remember the summer the crickets learned Morse and tapped our names until dawn?” I type “— — — ” and press send. Three dots appear, then vanish. The absence makes the code complete.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
